author: John T.
content: Reed Hastings is much more on the money than you are. Empathy never trumps talent or ability.

children.author: Chris
children.content:
Really? With that in mind, could you explain why does mother theresa or gandhi can make a huge impact in the real world? Does these people have so much money and talent to do so?

In my view, money and talent comes to play, but also emphathy.

children.author: Cory
children.content: Please expand your thought. How is Reed Hastings more on the money? In what ways are talent and ability a trump over empathy?

children.author: Sam
children.content:
Some people might excel at a company like Netflix. It certainly has an interesting philosophy, work culture, and approach thereto.

It’s not for everyone. I’m not sure I’d want to work there as an engineer. But also, I think Netflix does have an approach to empathy. Part of their philosophy is communicating to employees that “we’re not a family, we’re a team, and if we don’t need you anymore, it doesn’t mean you’re not awesome.” That’s empathy.

author: JPA
content: This feels spot on. In a dysfunctional organization where you can’t trust your coworkers, don’t feel safe to ask questions or get real answers, I’ve seen people toil in the dark until the breaking point where they quit. Attrition is organizationally expensive; retaining and supporting engineers, building a culture of collaboration and information sharing helps even the most dedicated engineers progress in their skills & careers, and builds an organization where people want to stay. Talent & ability don’t go very far if they continually pass through your organization on the way out the door, or cause others to do so.

author: Stephen
content: The way you talk about deriving process from values reminds me of the common dictum about refactoring: “add features or refactor, never both at the same time.” In the case of process, “derive process from values or change your values. Never drive values changes by changing process.” Does this fit with what you intended to say? I think it’s a cool analogy even though I’m sure it breaks down when looked at too closely.

author: mt
